perturbation.original_text: All obligations hereunder shall continue for five (5) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: All obligations hereunder shall continue indefinitely from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the confidentiality obligation from a fixed term of five years to "indefinitely" creates uncertainty and is potentially unenforceable under Kansas law, which favors reasonable and definite contract terms. Perpetual confidentiality is often disfavored by courts unless very narrowly defined.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Kansas law on contract enforceability requires reasonable and definite terms.
perturbation.law_citation: Campbell v. City of Beloit, 279 Kan. 398, 404, 109 P.3d 1184, 1188 (2005)
perturbation.law_explanation: Kansas contract law requires that contracts are not indefinite or unreasonably broad. Changing the term to "indefinitely" could make it unreasonably broad and thus unenforceable under Kansas law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Pittsburg State Univ. V. Kansas Bd. Of Regents :: 2005 :: Kansas Supreme Court Decisions :: Kansas Case Law :: Kansas Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Pittsburg State Univ. V. Kansas Bd. Of Regents Annotate this Case IN THE SUPREME COURT OF THE STATE OF KANSAS No. 91,305 PITTSBURG STATE UNIVERSITY/ KANSAS NATIONAL EDUCATION ASSOCIATION, Appellee, v. KANSAS BOARD OF REGENTS/PITTSBURG STATE UNIVERSITY and PUBLIC EMPLOYEE RELATIONS BOARD, Appellants. SYLLABUS BY THE COURT 1. Under the Public Employer-Employee Relations Act, K.S.A. 75-4321 et seq., the Kansas Board of Regents, a public employer, is required to meet and confer with a recognized public employee organization on the subject of the ownership of intellectual property only if ownership of intellectual property is a condition of employment. K.S.A. 75-4327. If the ownership of intellectual property is a condition of employment, the subject may be included in a memorandum of agreement if no exception applies, i.e., if ownership of intellectual property is not preempted by state or federal law, is not a right of a public employee, or is not a right of a public employer. K.S.A. 75-4330. 2. Neither state nor federal law preempts the subject of ownership of intellectual property from being the subject of negotiations between a public employer and a recognized public employee organization or from being included within the scope of a memorandum of understanding. Therefore, the subject of the ownership of intellectual property is not preempted within the meaning of K.S.A. 75-4330(a)(1), the applicable provision of the Public Employer-Employee Relations Act, K.S.A. 75-4321 et seq. Review of the judgment of the Court of Appeals in an unpublished opinion filed December 10, 2004. Appeal from Shawnee district court; CHARLES E. ANDREWS, JR., judge. Judgment of the Court of Appeals reversing the district court is reversed. Judgment of the district court is affirmed in part, reversed in part, and remanded with directions. Opinion filed November 10, 2005. William Scott Hesse, assistant at
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a five-year confidentiality obligation. The modified contract changes this to 'indefinitely.' The Perturbation Explanation indicates this indefinite term creates uncertainty and may be unenforceable under Kansas law, favoring definite contract terms. The Law Explanation supports this, stating Kansas contract law requires contracts not to be indefinite or unreasonably broad. Snippet 1, Pittsburg State Univ. V. Kansas Bd. Of Regents, indirectly supports this, as it highlights the importance of clear terms in contracts under Kansas law, especially concerning employment conditions, suggesting a general legal preference for clarity and defined terms in contractual obligations, which an 'indefinite' term violates. The change directly removes a definite term, replacing it with one likely to be unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the completion or termination of any discussions between the parties, or at any time within fourteen (14) days of receipt of a written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall (i) promptly return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information disclosed in tangible form and copies thereof; or (ii) promptly destroy such Confidential Information (including all copies thereof) and certify their destruction to the Disclosing Party .
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the completion or termination of any discussions between the parties, the Receiving Party may, at its sole discretion, return or destroy the Confidential Information disclosed in tangible form and copies thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to return or destroy confidential information and making it discretionary contradicts standard NDA practices and increases risk for the disclosing party. Furthermore, failure to reasonably protect confidential information could be considered a breach of duty of care, contradicting the purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of care in handling confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the return/destruction of confidential information discretionary, the modified text weakens the Receiving Party's duty of care, potentially leading to a breach of contract for failing to adequately protect the Disclosing Party's information. Section 323 establishes the general duty of care one party owes another.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the return or destruction of confidential information, ensuring protection for the disclosing party. The changed text makes this action discretionary, weakening the protection. The Law Explanation states that this weakens the Receiving Party's duty of care, potentially leading to a breach of contract. The perturbation explanation supports this further by stating failure to protect confidential information could be a breach of duty of care. Although the scraped snippets provide no information, the change from a required action to a discretionary one represents a significant shift away from standard NDA practices and therefore creates a legally significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will terminate two (2) years from the Effective Date, but may be terminated by either party at any time upon thi rty (30) days written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will terminate two (2) years from the Effective Date, and may only be terminated by YRC Freight at any time, without cause or notice.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the termination clause to allow only YRC Freight to terminate the agreement at any time without notice creates an unconscionable and potentially unenforceable contract. Kansas law requires contracts to have mutuality of obligation. This lacks that mutuality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Kansas law on mutuality of obligation in contracts.
perturbation.law_citation: Varney Business Services, Inc. v. Pottroff, 275 Kan. 20, 59 P.3d 1003 (2002)
perturbation.law_explanation: Kansas law requires mutuality of obligation for a contract to be enforceable. By only allowing YRC Freight to terminate the contract without cause or notice, the contract lacks mutuality and could be deemed unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction. The original text allows either party to terminate the agreement with 30 days' notice. The changed text allows only YRC Freight to terminate the agreement at any time without notice. The Perturbation Explanation states that this lacks mutuality of obligation, which Kansas law requires. The Law Explanation also states Kansas law requires mutuality of obligation for a contract to be enforceable and that the modified contract lacks mutuality, which would make it unenforceable. Because the changed text removes the ability of one party to terminate the contract, it contradicts the requirement for mutuality of obligation, making the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Any legal action or proceeding relating to this Agreement shall be instituted in a state or federal court in Johnson County, Kansas.
perturbation.changed_text: Any legal action or proceeding relating to this Agreement shall be instituted exclusively in the court of YRC Freight's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: This change attempts to eliminate the state and federal court in Johnson County, Kansas, and give YRC Freight the sole discretion in choosing the venue for legal proceedings. This is likely unenforceable as it violates the principle of fair venue selection and could be seen as an attempt to forum shop. This is in contradiction with the state laws regarding proper venue selection. Depending on the cause, you can't necessarily restrict it to only the company's preference.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Kansas venue rules for contract actions.
perturbation.law_explanation: K.S.A. 60-604 outlines the permissible venues for civil actions in Kansas. The changed text attempts to override these statutory provisions by granting YRC Freight the exclusive right to choose the venue, potentially conflicting with the statutory rules and undermining the fairness of the legal process for the other party.

Actions against corporations. An action against a domestic corporation, or against a foreign corporation which is qualified to do business in this state, other than an action for which venue is otherwise specifically prescribed by law, may be brought in the county in which: (1) Its registered office is located; (2) the cause of action arose; (3) the defendant is transacting business at the time of the filing of the petition, if the plaintiff is a resident of such county at the time the cause of action arose; (4) there is located tangible personal property which is the subject of an action for the possession thereof if immediate possession is sought in accordance with K.S.A. 60-1005 and amendments thereto at the time of the filing of the action; or (5) equipment or facilities for use in the supply of transportation services, or communication services, including, without limitation, telephonic communication services, are located, where the subject of such action relates to transportation services or communication services supplied or rendered, in whole or in part, using such equipment or facilities. History: L. 1963, ch. 303, 60-604; L. 1965, ch. 355, § 3; L. 1989, ch. 178, § 2; L. 1995, ch. 174, § 1; July 1. Prev Article 6. - VENUE Next 2025. Powered by KLISS. Rendered: 2025-07-13T03:25:09. Head Rev No: 1485631(I)
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates venue in Johnson County, Kansas. The changed text gives YRC Freight the exclusive right to choose the venue. K.S.A. 60-604, as detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifies permissible venues for actions against corporations in Kansas. By granting YRC Freight the exclusive right to choose the venue, the changed text directly contradicts the statute, potentially undermining the fairness of the legal process for the other party and conflicting with statutory venue rules.
